body:
My `.pyx` file uses a PEP-484 style type signature:
```
#cython: embedsignature=True
def f(path: str, a: int = 0, b: bool = True) -> typing.List[str]:
    return []
```
Cython 0.29.13 turns this into the docstring:
`f(unicode path: str, a: int = 0, bool b: bool = True) -> typing.List[str]`
(see also #1370 for "unicode")

When using mypy-stubgen, that docstring turns into:
`def f(unicodepath: str, a: int = ..., boolb: bool = ...) -> typing.List[str]: ...`

Which causes mypy false positives for Python code calling `f` with a named argument for `b`.

I think `embedsignature` should not output Cython-specific syntax, as that breaks tooling consuming these docstrings.
Ideally it'd also convert Cython signatures into the corresponding Python signatures; but it would be good enough for me if it just left PEP484 signatures as-is.

comment_data.body:
Alternate ideas:
 * Cython could maybe generate `.pyi` stubs itself?
 * I could enable `binding=True` to expose the signature to Python tooling. However the current `mypy-stubgen` only emits function declarations for what it considers a "C function":
```py
def is_c_function(obj: object) -> bool:
    return inspect.isbuiltin(obj) or type(obj) is type(ord)
```
so all Cython functions with binding enabled get converted to a simple `f: typing.Any`. This should probably be fixed on the mypy side.

If generating `.pyi` stubs is not too complex, Cython could do it automatically, yes, similar to how it now generates the embedded signatures in `AutoDocTransform`. PR welcome.

I also agree that generating 'normal' Py3 annotations would be nicer than generating Cython syntax. PR welcome for that, too.
Looking forward to this functionality! I usually write all documentation in `.pyx` files, so it could be very nice to have automatic `.pyi` generation!
@cmpute PR welcome, as I wrote. See the `AutoDocTransform`.

Note that mypy has a tool called `stubgen` to generate stub files from runtime information.

For Cython, it suffers from the fact that a few special methods like `__init__` and `__call__` [cannot expose their signatures as runtime type information](https://github.com/cython/cython/issues/3873). Apart from that, it [should generally work with Cython](https://github.com/python/mypy/issues/7542).

Over in PR #5415, we started a discussion about mixing Cython's C style type declarations with Python type annotations, which I'm generally opposed to due to the potential confusion that this causes. Let's continue the discussion in this issue since it leaves the scope of a PR.

An example is:
```cython
    @classmethod
    def c03(type cls: type[Foo], unsigned int a: int) -> float: return a
```
where the Cython type declarations (and note that this only works in Cython `.pyx` syntax) are more specific and concrete than the Python type annotations, but the latter can be used to generate user defined text signatures and `.pyi` stub files. The main issue here are the two audiences of the declarations. Python type annotations are geared towards humans, whereas the Cython C type declaration is meant for the compiler.

@dalcinl wrote:
> Remember that "Python type declarations" are actually just annotations.

… unless you use them as the way you declare types for Cython. In that case, they are type declarations, not just annotations. The fact that you can mix both in `.pyx` files is, well, limited to `.pyx` files. It doesn't help users who write their Cython code in `.py` modules to tell them that their life could be so much easier and cooler if they switched to non-Python syntax extensions and renamed their files. We are currently trying to discourage writing new code in Cython syntax and use Python syntax instead, simply because there is much more tooling available for writing Python code than for Cython code.

I don't think we should reject the mix of both as invalid syntax, but I also wouldn't want to present mixing both forms of type declarations as the best of both worlds. I don't want Python type annotations to be considered a second class citizen that is only looked at if there is nothing more convincing and can otherwise be happily ignored by the compiler. Any redundancy in code bares a potential risk of getting outdated, out of sync, and generally confusing. I consider duplicate type declarations something that Cython might warn about, not something to encourage.

@dalcinl suggested:
> ```python
> def foo(int arg: Annotated[int, MaxValueAllowed(42)]) -> Any:
>     ...
> ```

In Python syntax, you would spell it as follows:
```python
def foo(arg: Annotated[cython.int, MaxValueAllowed(42)]) -> Any:
    ...
```
I don't see why the two type declarations should be preferred over a single one here.

> I don't see why the two type declarations should be preferred over a single one here.

Unless you are using `#cython: annotation_typing=False` in a pyx file.
https://github.com/jon-edward/stubgen-pyx seems to be a new tool for generating pyi files from Cython code. Not sure how stable or complete it is though.

Another possible approach I've experimented with: use Cython's [pure python syntax](https://cython.readthedocs.io/en/latest/src/tutorial/pure.html), and directly consume the Cython source code in the type checking tool.

This has the major upside that the type checker can also partially check the Cython code itself.

Downsides of this approach:
 * some Cython features are not yet available in pure python syntax (e.g. C function pointer types or C++ reference types -- [pull request adding those](https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/7121))
 * in Cython 3.2 it is not possible to have generic `cdef` classes. There's an [open pull request](https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/7540) that would solve this.

> https://github.com/jon-edward/stubgen-pyx seems to be a new tool for generating pyi files from Cython code. Not sure how stable or complete it is though.

It looks like the cuda-Python project is experimenting with it a bit. We'll see how they get on... 

If it works well that'll be good - I'd be happy enough to be able to recommend a good tool rather than implement it ourselves.

> Another possible approach I've experimented with: use Cython's [pure python syntax](https://cython.readthedocs.io/en/latest/src/tutorial/pure.html), and directly consume the Cython source code in the type checking tool.

It's certainly one of the intentions of the pure-syntax that this kind of thing becomes easier. Although the downsides are as you point out.

rename.from: Embedded signature uses Cython syntax even if source code does not
rename.to: Generate Python typing compatible text signatures (and stubs)

title: Generate Python typing compatible text signatures (and stubs)
